skill.name: experiment-detail-comparator
skill.description: Compare experimental method details between two Zotero PDF papers, identify protocol differences (ratios, dosages, timing, conditions), search supporting literature to explain why they differ, and generate an HTML report. Use when you need a parameter-level comparison of two methods and evidence-backed reasons for discrepancies.
skill.summary: Compare experimental method parameters between two PDF papers and generate evidence-backed HTML reports
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found 1461 potential issues, but over 95 percent are false positives originating from temp/ scientific paper content and documentation code blocks. Confirmed risks include API credential access from Notes/.env (ZOTERO_API_KEY, MISTRAL_API_KEY), external command execution via subprocess for Zotero CLI, curl, and EDirect PubMed tools, network requests for PDF downloads and literature search, and filesystem operations for temp file creation and report generation. All elevated-risk operations are transparent, documented, and necessary for the skill's function of comparing experimental methods between PDF papers. No evidence of malicious intent, data exfiltration, or obfuscation was found in the actual code.

medium_findings.title: API Credential Access from .env Files
medium_findings.description: The skill reads ZOTERO_API_KEY and MISTRAL_API_KEY from Notes/.env files for authenticating with Zotero and Mistral PDF conversion services. This is necessary for the skill's core function but exposes API credentials if the .env file is compromised.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear evidence of .env file reading with documented API keys. Code at scripts/get_zotero_pdf.py:41-60 and scripts/convert_pdf_to_markdown.py:13-32 directly reads these keys for API authentication.

medium_findings.title: External Command Execution via subprocess
medium_findings.description: The skill executes external commands using subprocess.run for Zotero CLI tools, curl for API calls, pip for dependency installation, and EDirect for PubMed searches. Commands use argument lists (not shell=True) which mitigates command injection risk, but external tool access remains elevated risk.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Multiple subprocess.run calls confirmed in actual Python scripts, all with hardcoded command lists and no shell=True flag. Legitimate tool orchestration but represents elevated risk for an AI-executed skill.

medium_findings.title: Filesystem Write and Temp File Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill creates files in temp/ directory and writes processed JSON, markdown, and HTML report files. File copy operations using shutil and write operations are used throughout the pipeline.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed file write operations at multiple locations for legitimate PDF processing, JSON serialization, and HTML report generation.

low_findings.title: Network Requests to External APIs
low_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to download PDFs from Zotero API and conducts literature searches. URL construction uses configurable library_id and attachment_key parameters from user input and environment config.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network requests to api.zotero.org are legitimate and expected for the skill's function. Evidence shows standard REST API calls with documented authentication.

low_findings.title: Temp File Creation
low_findings.description: Temporary files are created in the system temp directory and local temp/ folder for interim processing artifacts such as downloaded PDFs and markdown conversions.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: tempfile.gettempdir() and local temp/ directory usage confirmed. Standard practice for a file-processing pipeline.

low_findings.title: Hidden File and Environment File Access
low_findings.description: The skill accesses Notes/.env and local Zotero storage directories for API credentials and PDF file discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed .env file access at scripts/get_zotero_pdf.py:48 and scripts/convert_pdf_to_markdown.py:20. Path is specific and documented.

dangerous_patterns.title: API Key Parsing from .env Files
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts parse ZOTERO_API_KEY and MISTRAL_API_KEY from plaintext .env files by reading file contents and splitting on = characters. Credentials are passed in subprocess command arguments via -H headers for curl, exposing them to process listing.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence of API keys read from .env and passed as curl -H header arguments, making them visible in process listings.

dangerous_patterns.title: subprocess.run for Tool Orchestration
dangerous_patterns.description: subprocess.run is used with hardcoded command lists (no shell=True) for curl API calls, pip dependency installs, and EDirect PubMed searches. While not command-injectable due to list-based invocation, external tool execution carries inherent risk in an AI skill context.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Multiple confirmed subprocess calls across 5 files. All use argument lists mitigating injection risk, but execution of external binaries from AI-driven workflows requires attention.

dangerous_patterns.title: Sensitive Filesystem Operations on .env and Zotero Storage
dangerous_patterns.description: Scripts directly read Notes/.env for API credentials and search local Zotero storage paths (~/Zotero/storage/) for PDF files with glob patterns.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct evidence of reading Notes/.env for credentials and searching Zotero storage directories. Operations are transparent and documented but represent sensitive filesystem access.

content.user_title: Compare Experimental Methods Between Research Papers
content.value_statement: Researchers often struggle to identify parameter-level differences between similar experimental protocols across papers. This skill solves that by extracting method details from two PDF papers, generating a structured comparison table, and searching the scientific literature for evidence-backed explanations of why the protocols differ.
actual_capabilities: Extract experimental parameters from PDF methods sections including concentrations, temperatures, durations, and dosages
Compare two research papers and identify specific protocol differences with structured diff output
Search PubMed and literature databases to find evidence-backed explanations for methodological differences
Generate formatted HTML comparison reports with highlighted differences and cited references
Retrieve PDF files from Zotero library using attachment keys or search queries
Extract structured parameter data from PDF tables using pdfplumber integration
limitations: Requires a Zotero library with attached PDF files for automated paper retrieval
Comparison quality depends on completeness of methods sections in the source papers
Cannot perform statistical re-analysis or numerical validation of experimental results
Literature search effectiveness depends on availability of external search tools like EDirect

use_cases.title: Reproducibility Audit for Lab Protocols
use_cases.description: Compare experimental methods across related publications to verify protocol consistency and identify undocumented parameter changes that could affect reproducibility.

use_cases.title: Protocol Optimization by Parameter Comparison
use_cases.description: Identify optimal experimental conditions by comparing dosage, timing, and temperature parameters between two papers using the same method type.

use_cases.title: Peer Review Methods Validation
use_cases.description: Cross-reference methodological claims in a manuscript against established protocols in the literature, with evidence-graded explanations for any discrepancies.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Two-Paper Comparison
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the experimental methods between the paper 'CRISPR-Cas9 knockout in HeLa cells' and 'Efficient genome editing in HEK293 cells'. List all parameter differences including concentrations, timing, and temperature.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick comparison of two papers for initial screening

prompt_templates.title: Comparison with Literature Search
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the methods used in paper1 and paper2 for the Western blot protocol. For each difference found in blocking buffer composition and primary antibody dilution, search PubMed for evidence explaining the rationale.
prompt_templates.scenario: Detailed method comparison with evidence-backed explanations

prompt_templates.title: Deep Parameter Extraction and Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Extract all experimental parameters from the methods sections of both papers, including reagent concentrations, incubation times, centrifuge speeds, and buffer compositions. Identify any parameters that differ by more than 10 percent between the two protocols.
prompt_templates.scenario: Comprehensive parameter extraction for protocol harmonization

prompt_templates.title: Full End-to-End Workflow with HTML Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the complete comparison pipeline for two papers on CRISPR editing in T cells. Retrieve PDFs from Zotero, extract methods, compare all parameters, search literature for explanations of each difference, and generate an HTML report with evidence grading and citations.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complete pipeline execution with deliverable HTML report

output_examples.input: Compare the ELISA protocols between two papers measuring cytokine levels in mouse serum.
output: Parameter comparison table showing: coating buffer composition (carbonate vs phosphate), incubation temperature (4C vs room temperature), blocking agent (BSA vs skim milk), detection antibody dilution (1:1000 vs 1:2000), and substrate incubation time (15 min vs 30 min).
Literature search results found 3 references explaining that the choice of blocking agent can affect signal-to-noise ratio in serum-based ELISA assays.
Overall evidence grades: 2 High confidence explanations, 1 Medium confidence explanation, 0 Low confidence explanations.

output_examples.input: Extract and compare the qPCR thermocycling conditions between two papers on gene expression analysis.
output: Step 1: Retrieved both PDFs from Zotero library and converted to markdown.
Step 2: Extracted method sections - Paper 1 uses SYBR Green with 40 cycles at 60C annealing, Paper 2 uses TaqMan with 45 cycles at 58C annealing.

Step 3: Literature search found 2 references confirming 58-60C annealing is standard for these primer sets, and cycle number differences do not affect relative quantification when efficiency is controlled.

best_practices: Ensure both target PDF files are attached in your Zotero library before starting the comparison workflow
Review the extracted method details for accuracy before relying on the comparison output, especially for complex multi-step protocols
Use specific search terms including author names and publication years to improve paper matching accuracy
anti_patterns: Do not compare papers that use entirely different experimental approaches as the comparison engine requires matching method types
Do not rely on extracted data without manual verification of critical values like drug concentrations and incubation times
Do not use vague search queries when retrieving papers as this may return irrelevant results and waste processing time

faq.question: What types of experiments can this skill compare?
faq.answer: The skill can compare any experimental method where you have two PDF papers. It works best with highly similar protocols such as CRISPR editing, ELISA, Western blot, qPCR, cell culture protocols, and synthesis procedures.

faq.question: Do I need a Zotero account to use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes, the default workflow uses Zotero for PDF storage and retrieval. You need a Zotero account with papers added and PDF files attached. The skill can also process local PDF files directly.

faq.question: What API keys are required?
faq.answer: You need a Zotero API key for accessing your Zotero web library and a Mistral API key for the PDF-to-markdown conversion. Configure these in a Notes/.env file.

faq.question: Can I compare papers without Zotero?
faq.answer: Yes, you can provide local PDF file paths directly to the run_comparison.py script. The Zotero integration is optional for cases where PDFs are already available on your system.

faq.question: How does the literature search for explanations work?
faq.answer: The skill uses EDirect tools from NCBI to search PubMed for relevant literature. It formulates search queries based on the parameter differences found and grades the evidence quality as High, Medium, or Low.

faq.question: What output formats are supported?
faq.answer: The skill generates a structured HTML comparison report with highlighted differences, graded evidence sections, and citations. Intermediate outputs include JSON method files and markdown conversions for review.
